input.background: An economist wants to compare the mean dollar amounts owed by delinquent credit card customers across three socioeconomic classes (lower, middle, and upper class). A sample of 10 customers with delinquent accounts is selected from each group, and the amount owed by each is recorded.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline \multicolumn{3}{|l|}{Table 12.3 Dollars Owed, Example 12.9} \\
\hline Group 1 (lower class) & Group 2 (middle class) & Group 3 (upper class) \\
\hline \$148 & \$513 & \$335 \\
\hline 76 & 264 & 643 \\
\hline 393 & 433 & 216 \\
\hline 520 & 94 & 536 \\
\hline 236 & 535 & 128 \\
\hline 134 & 327 & 723 \\
\hline 55 & 214 & 258 \\
\hline 166 & 135 & 380 \\
\hline 415 & 280 & 594 \\
\hline 153 & 304 & 465 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: Fit the model to the data and give the least squares prediction equation.

output.answer: The least squares estimates of the $\beta$ 's are obtained from the sample means associated with the different levels of the qualitative variable. The least squares prediction equation is: $$\hat{y}=229.6+80.3 x_1+198.2 x_2.$$ Interpretations: - $\hat{\beta}_0=229.6$: The estimated mean delinquent amount for the lower-class group. - $\hat{\beta}_1=80.3$: The difference between the sample mean delinquent amounts for the middle- and lower-class groups ($309.9-229.6$). - $\hat{\beta}_2=198.2$: The difference between the sample mean delinquent amounts for the upper- and lower-class groups ($427.8-229.6$).

meta_info.caption: A Model with One Qualitative Independent Variable-Credit Card Debt
